specification.topic-statement: The history persistence system loads command history from a JSONL file at startup and appends new entries to that file when commands are submitted.

scope.in-scope: File path resolution, directory creation, JSONL parsing and validation during load, capacity enforcement during load, entry formatting and writing during append, and graceful degradation on I/O errors.
scope.boundary:
This specification covers the file I/O layer only. The in-memory history structure that receives loaded entries and supplies entries to be appended is covered in the Command History specification.
The in-memory history structure maintains an ordered list up to a configured capacity. Loaded entries are submitted to it one by one in chronological order. If the number of valid entries in the file exceeds the capacity, only the most recent entries are submitted (oldest are discarded at load time before submission).

field.name: cmd
field.note: the full command string exactly as submitted

field.name: ts
field.note: UTC timestamp in ISO-8601 format: YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SSZ

field.name: encoding
field.note: JSONL (JSON Lines): one JSON object per line, terminated by a newline character

field.name: path
field.note: .ikigai/history relative to the current working directory; not configurable

behavior.directory-management:
Before any file operation, the `.ikigai` directory is ensured to exist.
If the directory already exists, no action is taken. If the path exists but is not a directory (e.g., it is a plain file), an error is returned.
If the directory does not exist, it is created with permissions allowing owner read, write, and execute, and group and other read and execute (`0755`). If creation fails for any reason other than the directory having been created by another process between the existence check and the creation attempt, an error is returned. The race condition case — where the directory appears between the check and the creation — is treated as success.
behavior.loading:
History is loaded at startup by reading and parsing the history file.
If the history file does not exist, an empty file is created at the history file path and loading completes with no entries. The empty file creation ensures that subsequent existence checks succeed.
If the file exists but contains no bytes, loading completes with no entries. No entries are submitted to the in-memory structure.
If the file contains data, it is read in full and split into lines at newline characters. Empty lines are silently skipped.
Each non-empty line is parsed as JSON. If any of the following conditions applies, the line is skipped and a warning is recorded; no error is returned and parsing continues with the next line:
The line is not valid JSON.
The JSON root value is not an object.
The JSON object does not contain a `cmd` field, or the `cmd` field's value is not a string.
If the line is valid and the `cmd` field is a non-empty string, the command value is extracted. The `ts` field and any other fields are not examined and do not affect whether the line is accepted.
The timestamp field written during append is never read during load. It is present in the format but is entirely ignored by the parser.
After all lines have been parsed, the collection of successfully extracted command strings is compared against the history capacity. If the number of valid entries exceeds the capacity, the oldest entries (those appearing earliest in the file) are discarded until the count equals the capacity. Only the most recent entries are submitted to the in-memory structure.
Entries are submitted to the in-memory history in chronological order, oldest first, so that the most recently submitted command ends up as the most recent entry.
A failure to create the history directory, or a failure to read the file after it is known to exist, produces an error. In both cases, the calling system logs the error but continues operating without history from the file. The application does not abort.
Individual malformed lines do not produce errors; they produce warnings. Loading always succeeds unless the file cannot be read at all.
behavior.appending:
A command string is appended to the history file each time a command is submitted.
The entry is formatted as a JSON object containing two fields: `cmd` holds the command string, and `ts` holds the current UTC time formatted as an ISO-8601 timestamp (`YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SSZ`). The formatted object is written to the file followed by a newline character.
The file is opened in append mode. The formatted JSON line is written and the file is closed. No explicit flush or synchronization to disk is performed beyond what the operating system provides on close.
A process crash after the file is opened but before the write completes can produce a partial line at the end of the file. The partial line will be rejected as malformed JSON on the next load and will be silently skipped.
No file locking is performed. If multiple processes append to the same history file simultaneously, the resulting file may contain entries in a non-chronological order relative to when they were submitted, but both entries will be present and parseable.
A failure to create the directory, open the file, format the entry, or write to the file produces an error. The calling system logs the error but continues operating. The in-memory history is not affected; only the file write is skipped.
